pursuitEconomics.content:
Compensation & Equity Upside Assessment

Sigma Computing Series D unicorn status suggests significant equity optionality. Group PM (Director+ equivalent) roles at $100M ARR companies typically offer $200–260K base + 20–40% bonus + 0.2–0.5% equity (valuation dependent on Series D round and strike price). Equity remains meaningful given unicorn status and clear path to Series E/IPO within 3–5 years. Unlike public company roles, Sigma offers execution upside and earlier-stage growth momentum. Comp is lower than FAANG but significantly higher than Series A/B startups. Key consideration: Series D valuations have compressed in 2025/2026 due to public market headwinds and higher WACC; ensure equity modeling accounts for potential down-round scenarios in Series E. Risk/reward profile is excellent for someone seeking meaningful equity ownership without full startup volatility.

  • Base comp: $220–260K; bonus 20–40%; equity ~0.3% (valuation-dependent)
  • Equity upside: Strong contingent on Series E and eventual liquidity event (3–5 years)
  • Market timing: Late-stage but pre-IPO; better risk/reward than early-stage, more upside than public

existence.content:
Why This Role Exists Now

Sigma Computing is scaling from $100M ARR toward $300M+ (Series E/IPO target). AI is the fastest-growing use case in business intelligence (generative SQL, natural language queries, automated insights). Competitors (Tableau, Power BI, Looker, Qlik) are all embedding AI; Sigma's differentiation depends on superior AI/ML integration and user experience. A Group PM, AI role signals executive commitment to AI as a first-class product pillar, not an afterthought. Sigma is likely consolidating multiple AI initiatives (NLQ, automated insights, copilot features) under one leader to ensure coherent strategy, cross-team execution, and clear roadmap prioritization. Additionally, Sigma's rapid headcount growth (from 200 to 500–1K in last 18 months) requires organizational clarity; GPM roles establish clear ownership and reduce cross-functional friction. This role likely reports to Chief Product Officer or VP Product, and owns AI product strategy, customer research, and metrics for AI-driven features across the platform.

  • Why now: AI is existential competitive differentiator; consolidation of initiatives required
  • Scope: Likely owning AI product strategy, multiple PM team, and cross-org roadmap prioritization
  • Growth stage: Scale-up navigating complexity; clear need for org clarity and specialist leaders

companyMarket.content:
Company Market & Competitive Position

Sigma Computing operates in the $60B+ global BI/analytics market. Core positioning: cloud-native BI for data teams and business users. Competitive landscape: Tableau (Salesforce-owned, dominant installed base but perceived as legacy), Power BI (Microsoft, strong in enterprise + SMB), Looker (Google Cloud, strong in data dev community), and emerging competitors (Qlik, Sisense, Thoughtspot, Metabase). Sigma's differentiation: cloud-native architecture, ease of use for non-technical users, and SQL-first design (appeals to data engineers). AI is the strategic battleground—NLQ (natural language query) to SQL, automated insight generation, and anomaly detection are table-stakes. Sigma's challenge: competing against Tableau/Power BI's massive installed bases while proving superior AI quality and UX. Addressable market spans SMB analytics (high growth, price-sensitive) to enterprise (lower growth, feature-rich, switching costs). Sigma's GTM currently targets mid-market and emerging enterprises; competing upmarket in 2026/2027 will require proven AI capabilities.

  • TAM: $60B+ global BI/analytics; Sigma estimated 2–3% share
  • Competitive threats: Tableau dominance + Microsoft Power BI; niche players (Qlik, Thoughtspot) in adjacent segments
  • Strategic priority: AI differentiation, ease-of-use leadership, enterprise expansion

plausible.content:
Realistic Requirements & Expectations

Sigma seeks a Group PM, AI candidate with 5–8+ years of PM experience, ideally including 2–3 years in AI/ML product or analytics product leadership. Preferred background: B2B SaaS product leadership in data, analytics, ML platforms, or infrastructure; demonstrated ability to ship AI/ML features and manage ambiguity (AI has higher failure rate than traditional features). Nice-to-haves: previous PM role at unicorn or growth-stage company (comfort with scale and organizational complexity), BI/analytics domain expertise (Tableau, Looker, Power BI background), or successful team leadership (3+ PMs). Sigma will look for: strategic thinking (how does AI fit into long-term product vision?), customer empathy (understand data team and business user personas), and data literacy (understand data quality, model hallucinations, ethical implications of AI). Given Sigma's growth stage, they'll prioritize execution bias and ability to operate in ambiguous, fast-moving environment over deep domain expertise. MBA is common but not required.

  • Must-haves: 5–8+ years PM; AI/ML product experience or analytics product background
  • Preferred: Unicorn/growth-stage experience, team leadership, strategic thinking on AI strategy
  • Nice-to-haves: BI/analytics domain, data literacy, cross-functional AI project shipping

screenOut.content:
Disqualification Risks & Red Flags

Sigma screens out candidates lacking SaaS product leadership experience or AI/ML product intuition. Red flags: primarily startup-only background (may struggle with scale and organizational complexity at Sigma), no shipped AI/ML features or misunderstanding of AI/ML capabilities/limitations, or limited analytics/data domain knowledge (learnable but shows less credibility). Candidates uncomfortable with rapid change, organizational ambiguity, or cross-functional influence will struggle in growth-stage environment. Over-leveled compensation expectations ($300K+ base) or candidates primarily seeking founder/exec track may deprioritize growth PM roles. Lack of strategic thinking or tendency to optimize locally (single feature) vs. globally (product strategy) is disqualifying. Candidates perceived as data-illiterate or unable to understand technical constraints of AI/ML models will lack credibility with engineering and data science teams. Finally, candidates without demonstrated ability to manage competing stakeholders (data engineers vs. business users vs. executives) will struggle with BI product complexity.

  • Experience gaps: No SaaS or analytics background; startup-only trajectory
  • AI/ML literacy: No shipped AI features; misunderstanding of ML model capabilities, hallucination, ethical risks
  • Domain expertise: Data illiteracy; inability to understand SQL, data pipelines, or BI workflows
  • Strategic thinking: Local optimization vs. global product strategy; unable to articulate multi-year vision

needs.content:
What Sigma Really Needs (Beneath the Job Description)

Sigma's hidden need is a PM who can navigate the AI hype/reality gap without overselling. AI is central to fundraising narrative and competitive positioning, but AI features have higher failure rates, longer engineering cycles, and user adoption challenges vs. traditional BI features. Sigma needs someone to honestly assess which AI initiatives have real customer value vs. which are "checkboxes for investors". They need a leader who can set clear success metrics for AI features (not just shipping, but adoption, retention, revenue impact). Internally, they need someone to manage the tension between data engineers/data scientists (who have high quality/accuracy standards) and product/marketing teams pushing for faster AI feature shipping. They need a cross-org orchestrator who can resolve prioritization conflicts between AI roadmap and core BI product. They also need a customer research rigor leader; many BI vendors are building AI features in isolation from actual user workflows. Finally, they need someone who can defend against the creeping complexity risk—adding AI features without careful consideration of UX, documentation, support burden, and training requirements.

  • Real need #1: Navigate AI hype/reality gap; honest assessment of feature ROI and customer value
  • Real need #2: Set clear success metrics for AI features; distinguish shipping from adoption/impact
  • Real need #3: Bridge data science/engineering rigor with product/marketing velocity pressure
  • Real need #4: Customer research discipline; ensure AI features solve real user problems, not investor checkboxes

fit.content:
Ili's Fit: Strengths & Natural Advantages

Ili's 15+ years in ad tech, data/ML platforms, and B2B SaaS map directly to Sigma's needs. Data/ML platform background is a perfect match for understanding AI/ML product nuance, customer research rigor, and ability to bridge engineering/data science with product/marketing. Ad tech experience demonstrates comfort with large-scale data systems, customer segmentation, and metrics discipline—critical for BI product. B2B SaaS expertise ensures understanding of enterprise go-to-market, contract value, and expansion revenue strategies. Ili's combination of technical (ML platform) and consumer/commercial (ad tech, SaaS) product experience gives a rare vantage point for evaluating AI/ML feature ROI and user adoption dynamics. Ili has likely built AI/ML features, managed customer research with technical users, and navigated the hype/reality gap—exactly what Sigma needs. Ili's metric discipline and customer empathy are direct strengths. Additionally, if Ili has led PM teams or coordinated across engineering/data science/product, that cross-functional leadership directly applies to Group PM scope at Sigma.

  • Data/ML platform intuition: Direct match for AI/ML product category and technical depth
  • Scale experience: Ad tech background demonstrates comfort with large-scale systems and data complexity
  • Metrics discipline: Proven track record measuring feature ROI, customer adoption, and revenue impact
  • Cross-functional leadership: ML platform work requires bridging engineering/data science/product/marketing

gaps.content:
Gaps & Mitigation Strategies

Ili's primary gap is likely BI/analytics domain expertise. Mitigate by: (1) Spending 4–5 hours using Sigma Computing, Tableau, and Looker directly—learn core workflows, understand user personas (data engineers vs. analysts vs. executives), (2) Scheduling 4–5 customer conversations with analytics teams at Ili's network—understand their Sigma evaluation, competitive alternatives, AI feature desires, (3) Reading 2–3 analyst reports (Gartner Magic Quadrant BI, Forrester Wave) to understand competitive positioning and buyer priorities, (4) Learning basic SQL and dbt/data warehouse concepts to build credibility with technical teams. A secondary gap may be limited BI ecosystem knowledge (data warehousing integration, dbt, data governance tools); this is learnable but shows deeper domain investment if researched pre-interview. Ili's brand equity from ML platforms + data tech background likely outweighs domain gaps—Sigma values operators who understand AI/ML rigor and customer research over pure BI experts. Lead with metric discipline, cross-functional leadership, and AI feature ROI thinking; frame domain learning as priority and show evidence of active learning (customer conversations, product usage).

  • Gap #1—BI/analytics domain: Mitigate via hands-on product usage, customer conversations, analyst reading
  • Gap #2—Data ecosystem: Mitigate via learning basic SQL/dbt, data warehouse concepts; show curiosity
  • Mitigation strategy: Lead with AI/ML and metrics discipline; frame domain learning as active priority

network.content:
Network Strategy & Warm Introductions

Identify warm intros via: (1) Former colleagues or investors in data/ML/analytics space who may know Sigma founders or investors (Sigma raised Series D from Salesforce Ventures, Sequoia, Greylock; check if Ili has VC connections), (2) Current Sigma PM/engineering leaders on LinkedIn—search "Sigma Computing Product Manager" + connections; identify 2–3 PM peers for intel gathering, (3) Data/analytics leaders at Ili's prior companies (Stripe, Shopify, HubSpot, etc.) who may be Sigma customers or have evaluated Sigma; ask for introduction, (4) Venture partners or hiring recruiters specializing in growth-stage analytics companies. Most powerful move: Identify a current Sigma PM (not necessarily AI-focused) and request a 20-minute conversation about org structure, PM culture, and hiring priorities. Use that conversation to gather intel, then leverage in hiring manager outreach. Sigma is well-funded and scaled; internal referrals significantly accelerate hiring process. If Ili has shipped AI/ML features at prior companies, one reference from an engineering leader (data scientist, ML engineer) vouching for Ili's collaboration and product intuition is gold.

  • Highest-leverage intros: Sigma investor connections (Salesforce Ventures, Sequoia, Greylock); current Sigma PM peers
  • Secondary paths: Analytics/data leaders at Ili's network; VC recruiters specializing in analytics
  • Proof point: Engineering/data science reference vouching for Ili's cross-functional collaboration

interview.content:
Interview Preparation & Key Narratives

Prepare to discuss: (1) An AI/ML feature Ili shipped—walk through customer research, technical constraints, go-to-market strategy, and actual adoption/revenue outcome; emphasize how Ili managed the hype/reality gap and set honest success metrics, (2) A time Ili led cross-functional collaboration between technical teams (engineers, data scientists) and commercial teams (product, marketing, sales); highlight conflict resolution and decision-making under ambiguity, (3) How Ili thinks about analytics/BI from user perspective—specific workflows, personas (data engineers vs. business users), and pain points; show customer empathy and domain learning, (4) Metric discipline: example of a feature Ili shipped, measured rigorously, and made hard go/no-go decisions based on data, not hype. Expect questions on AI strategy (where should Sigma focus AI investment?), competitive positioning vs. Tableau/Power BI, and organizational structure for managing AI product. Have 3–5 thoughtful questions on Sigma's AI vision, customer research process, and success metrics for AI initiatives. Avoid appearing like a pure data scientist or pure PM; Sigma wants someone who bridges both worlds.

  • Story #1: AI/ML feature shipped; customer research, technical constraints, honest success metrics
  • Story #2: Cross-functional collaboration between technical and commercial teams; conflict resolution
  • Story #3: Analytics/BI domain learning; customer personas and pain points
  • Story #4: Metric discipline; shipped feature, rigorous measurement, go/no-go decisions

proofPoints.content:
Proof Points & Credibility Anchors

Ili should lead with: (1) Named AI/ML features shipped with quantified outcomes (e.g., "Led natural language analytics feature; achieved 45% adoption in first 6 months, drove $2M ARR expansion, 20% improvement in customer NRR"), (2) Metrics discipline examples: specific features measured rigorously, A/B tests run, customer cohort analysis driving decisions, (3) Cross-functional leadership: names of data scientists, engineers, go-to-market leaders influenced; specific decisions driven through collaboration, (4) Customer research depth: win/loss analysis, customer interview cadence, user research methodology for complex technical products, (5) Scale examples: number of customers in target segment, ARR managed, geographic or product expansion driven by PM decisions, (6) Team leadership (if applicable): number of PMs hired/mentored, retention rates, org structure built. Bring references from engineering/data science leaders who can vouch for Ili's technical credibility and cross-functional collaboration. Bring customer references from analytics/data roles who can speak to Ili's understanding of their workflows and pain points. Data-driven proof points matter more than credentials in late-stage growth company PM roles.

  • Quantified wins: AI/ML features shipped, adoption rates, revenue/ARR impact, NRR improvement
  • Metrics discipline: Examples of rigorous measurement, A/B tests, go/no-go decisions based on data
  • Cross-functional influence: Names and titles of engineering/data science leaders influenced; specific decisions
  • Customer research: Win/loss examples, customer interview cadence, understanding of data team workflows

dealBreaker.content:
Potential Deal Breakers & Why It Might Not Work

Key risks: (1) Sigma's growth and scale means rapid organizational changes—team org structures, reporting lines, priorities shift frequently; if Ili requires stability or clear roadmaps, this environment may chafe, (2) AI hype at Sigma could outpace reality; if AI initiatives underperform or customer adoption is low, Ili may inherit blame despite strategic excellence, or conversely, be blamed for "not delivering" despite realistic constraints, (3) Sigma's competitive moat vs. Tableau/Power BI is unclear; despite better UX or AI, winning market share against entrenched platforms is difficult; if Ili is seeking a role with clear path to market leadership, this may be riskier than appears, (4) Series D valuations have compressed; if liquidity event is delayed beyond 5 years or down-round occurs in Series E, equity upside diminishes significantly, (5) Sigma's integration with Salesforce (post-acquisition or investment) could redirect product strategy or accelerate TMO (time-to-offer) timeline, reducing execution runway for PM initiatives.

  • Org volatility: Assess comfort with rapid change, org restructures, shifting priorities
  • AI realism: Understand that AI features may underperform investor expectations; prepare for realistic ROI
  • Competitive pressure: Research market dynamics; confirm confidence in ability to compete vs. Tableau/Power BI
  • Equity valuation: Understand Series D strike price and Series E timeline/scenario planning

nextSteps.content:
Action Items for This Week

By Tuesday: (1) Download and use Sigma Computing, Tableau, Looker for 2–3 hours each; understand core workflows and UI/UX differences, (2) Read Gartner Magic Quadrant BI 2025 and Forrester Wave BI reports to understand competitive positioning, (3) Schedule 4 customer conversations with analytics leaders at Ili's network—ask about Sigma evaluation, AI feature desires, competitive alternatives. By Thursday: (4) LinkedIn search "Sigma Computing Product Manager" and identify 1–2 PM peers for 20-minute intel calls, (5) Draft positioning doc: Ili's thesis on Sigma's AI strategy and GPM role contribution over next 18 months; show strategic thinking and domain learning, (6) Research Sigma's Series D round (Salesforce Ventures, Sequoia, Greylock investors); identify warm intro paths via VC partners. Next week: (7) Execute peer conversation with current Sigma PM to gather org structure and hiring intel, (8) Submit application or execute warm intro to CPO/VP Product, (9) Prepare core interview narratives: AI feature shipped, cross-functional leadership, BI domain learning, metrics discipline.

  • Domain immersion: Hands-on product usage + 4 customer conversations + sector reading by Thursday
  • Org intel: 1 peer conversation to understand Sigma org structure and success metrics
  • Strategic positioning: 1-pager on Sigma's AI strategy and GPM role contribution
